content.text: (also called a variational posterior or approximate posterior) is a tractable probability distribution
that is used to approximate an intractable true posterior
in Bayesian inference. The word "variational" comes from the calculus of variations: we are minimizing a functional (the KL divergence) over a
The core problem being solved: in generative models, we want to know the distribution of latent variables

attrs.latex: p(z \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid z)\, p(z)}{p(x)} = \frac{p(x \mid z)\, p(z)}{\int p(x \mid z)\, p(z)\, dz}

content.text: is a high-dimensional integral over all possible latent codes. For any reasonably expressive generative model, this integral is analytically intractable — it cannot be computed in closed form. Variational inference replaces the intractable true posterior with a
and optimizes the parameters to make the approximation as accurate as possible.

Variational inference underpins a large family of generative models — most importantly, Variational Autoencoders (VAEs). Without a way to handle intractable posteriors, the training of VAEs would require Monte Carlo estimation of the exact posterior, which is computationally prohibitive at the scale of modern datasets.

A concrete example of why this matters: suppose you want to build a generative model of handwritten digits (MNIST). You choose a latent variable model where each digit is generated from a 20-dimensional latent code
. To train this model, you need to compute

. The true posterior is a complex 20-dimensional distribution with no analytical form. Without variational inference, you would need to run MCMC for every training example — making training thousands of times slower and infeasible for datasets larger than a few hundred examples.

The variational distribution solves this by replacing the intractable posterior with a diagonal Gaussian parametrized by a neural network (the encoder). Training then reduces to optimizing a tractable lower bound (the ELBO) using standard gradient descent. This is why VAEs scale to ImageNet-sized datasets and are practical for production use.
: the variational distribution is chosen to be analytically tractable (e.g., diagonal Gaussian), making the ELBO computable and its gradient estimable via automatic differentiation.
: a single encoder network computes variational parameters for every data point, avoiding per-sample optimization. At test time, one forward pass produces the approximate posterior — O(1) inference.
: the reparametrisation trick makes sampling from the variational distribution differentiable, enabling gradient-based optimization through stochastic samples.
: unlike autoencoders that map each input to a single latent code, variational inference maps inputs to a
over latent codes. This uncertainty quantification is useful in downstream tasks (e.g., active learning, anomaly detection).
: once the model is trained, generating new samples requires sampling
from the prior and decoding — no inversion problem or approximate inference required at generation time.
: the variational distribution framework extends to hierarchical VAEs, flow-based posteriors, and discrete latent variables (using Gumbel-Softmax), enabling progressively richer generative models.

: because the same encoder predicts variational parameters for all inputs, it cannot perfectly model every individual posterior. The amortisation gap (the difference between the optimal per-sample ELBO and the amortised ELBO) is always > 0, leading to a looser bound.

: the diagonal Gaussian mean-field family cannot capture correlations between latent dimensions. If the true posterior has strong correlations, the variational approximation is poor, leading to suboptimal representations.
: with powerful decoders, the KL term dominates and q collapses to the prior, removing all information from the latent code. Requires careful mitigation (KL annealing, free bits).
ELBO is a lower bound, not the true log-likelihood
: maximizing the ELBO does not guarantee maximizing the true log-likelihood. The gap between them equals the KL divergence from the approximate to the true posterior.

: the reverse KL divergence (used in VI) is mode-seeking: it concentrates on high-probability regions of the true posterior, potentially missing important modes. Forward KL (used in MCMC) is mass-covering but intractable.

When the true posterior p(z|x) is intractable, variational inference replaces it with a simpler distribution q_φ(z|x) that is easy to sample from and evaluate. The variational distribution is chosen from a parametric family and its parameters φ are tuned to minimise the KL divergence from q to the true posterior — equivalently, to maximise the ELBO.
The family Q must balance two competing pressures: it should be flexible enough to approximate the true posterior well, but tractable enough to compute the ELBO and its gradients.
The most common choice is the mean-field family: the variational distribution fully factorises over the latent dimensions:

Each factor qᵢ has its own parameters φᵢ. For a Gaussian mean-field approximation, q_φ(z|x) = N(μ_φ(x), diag(σ²_φ(x))): a diagonal Gaussian with mean and variance that depend on x. This is the encoder in a VAE.

In classical variational inference, the variational parameters φ are optimised separately for each data point x — this is expensive for large datasets. Amortised variational inference instead trains a neural network (the encoder/inference network) to predict φ as a function of x:

The same encoder is reused across all data points. At test time, encoding a new x requires one forward pass — no per-sample optimisation. The trade-off is the amortisation gap: a shared network cannot perfectly model every individual posterior, so the ELBO is slightly looser than per-sample VI.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class GaussianEncoder(nn.Module):
    """Amortised variational encoder: x → (μ_z, log σ²_z)."""
    def __init__(self, x_dim, hidden_dim, z_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.shared = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(x_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
        )
        self.mu_head      = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, z_dim)
        self.log_var_head = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, z_dim)

    def forward(self, x):
        h          = self.shared(x)
        mu         = self.mu_head(h)
        log_var    = self.log_var_head(h)
        return mu, log_var

    def sample(self, x):
        """Reparametrised sample z ~ q_φ(z|x)."""
        mu, log_var = self.forward(x)
        eps = torch.randn_like(mu)
        return mu + torch.exp(0.5 * log_var) * eps

# Usage
enc = GaussianEncoder(x_dim=784, hidden_dim=512, z_dim=20)
x   = torch.randn(64, 784)
mu_z, log_var_z = enc(x)
print(mu_z.shape, log_var_z.shape)   # (64, 20), (64, 20)

To backpropagate gradients through a sample z ~ q_φ(z|x) = N(μ, σ²I), you cannot differentiate through the sampling step directly. The reparametrisation trick re-expresses z as a deterministic function of φ and a noise variable ε:

Now z is differentiable with respect to φ: ∂z/∂μ = 1 and ∂z/∂σ = ε. Gradients flow back through z into the encoder parameters without needing to differentiate through the distribution itself. This works for any location-scale family (Gaussian, Laplace, logistic); for discrete latent variables, the Gumbel-Softmax trick provides a differentiable approximation.

A failure mode specific to variational inference is posterior collapse: q_φ(z|x) collapses to the prior p(z), so the encoder ignores x and the latent code carries no information. This happens when the decoder is powerful enough to reconstruct x from the prior distribution alone — the KL term in the ELBO is minimised at zero with no reconstruction cost.
weight the reconstruction term higher: ELBO = E_q[log p(x|z)] − β KL with β < 1.
start with β=0 and linearly increase to 1 during training.
allow a fixed number of KL nats "for free" before penalising — each latent dimension is not penalised until its KL exceeds a threshold λ.
constraining the decoder capacity forces it to use z.

def elbo_with_free_bits(recon_loss, mu, log_var, free_bits=0.5):
    """
    ELBO loss with per-dimension free-bits regularisation.
    Each latent dim is only penalised once its KL exceeds `free_bits` nats.
    """
    # Per-dimension KL: (batch, z_dim)
    kl_per_dim = 0.5 * (log_var.exp() + mu**2 - 1 - log_var)
    # Threshold: allow free_bits per dimension
    kl_per_dim = torch.clamp(kl_per_dim, min=free_bits)
    kl = kl_per_dim.sum(dim=1).mean()
    return recon_loss + kl

attrs.alt: VAE architecture showing the variational distribution flow: encoder produces q_φ(z|x), reparametrization trick produces differentiable z samples, decoder reconstructs x, with KL regularization toward prior p(z)

content.text: The diagram illustrates the complete VAE inference and generation pipeline. The inference path (left to right, top): input x is processed by the encoder network to produce the parameters of the variational distribution q_φ(z|x) — a diagonal Gaussian with mean μ_φ(x) and variance σ²_φ(x). A sample z is drawn using the reparametrization trick (z = μ + σ⊙ε, where ε ~ N(0,I)), which makes the sampling step differentiable. The decoder maps z back to a reconstruction x̂. The prior p(z) = N(0,I) regularizes the approximate posterior via the KL divergence term in the ELBO, shown at the bottom of the diagram.
You need to train a latent variable generative model and the posterior is intractable (which is almost always the case for non-linear decoders).
You need fast (amortised) posterior inference at test time — one forward pass is required, not per-sample MCMC iterations.
The model needs to be trained end-to-end with gradient descent — variational inference provides a differentiable training objective via the reparametrisation trick.
You want the latent space to be structured and continuous (useful for interpolation, semantic arithmetic, and disentangled representations).
You are building downstream applications that need latent code uncertainty estimates (anomaly detection, semi-supervised learning, active learning).

: if you do not need fast inference and accuracy is paramount, Markov Chain Monte Carlo (especially HMC/NUTS) provides asymptotically exact posterior samples. Suitable for small-scale Bayesian inference problems.
: linear Gaussian models have closed-form posteriors; use the Kalman filter or conjugate distributions directly instead of variational inference.
You only need generation, not inference

: diffusion models and normalizing flows generate high-quality samples without requiring an approximate posterior. They achieve better sample quality than VAEs for image generation at the cost of slower sampling.
: standard VAEs with diagonal Gaussian posteriors have limited disentanglement. Consider flow-based posteriors (IAF-VAE, NVAE) or disentanglement-targeted objectives (beta-VAE, FactorVAE).
Below is a complete, runnable VAE implementation with proper ELBO loss, training loop, and generation. This illustrates all components of the variational distribution in practice.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from torchvision import datasets, transforms
import numpy as np

# ─── Model ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

class VAE(nn.Module):
    """
    Variational Autoencoder with diagonal Gaussian variational distribution.
    Trained on 28×28 grayscale images (MNIST).
    """
    def __init__(self, x_dim: int = 784, hidden_dim: int = 400, z_dim: int = 20):
        super().__init__()
        self.z_dim = z_dim

        # Encoder: x → (μ_z, log σ²_z)
        self.encoder_hidden = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(x_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
        )
        self.mu_layer      = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, z_dim)
        self.log_var_layer = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, z_dim)

        # Decoder: z → x̂
        self.decoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(z_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, x_dim),
            nn.Sigmoid(),  # pixel values in [0, 1]
        )

    def encode(self, x: torch.Tensor):
        """Compute variational distribution parameters q_φ(z|x) = N(μ, diag σ²)."""
        h        = self.encoder_hidden(x)
        mu       = self.mu_layer(h)
        log_var  = self.log_var_layer(h)
        return mu, log_var

    def reparametrize(self, mu: torch.Tensor, log_var: torch.Tensor):
        """
        Reparametrisation trick: z = μ + σ ⊙ ε, ε ~ N(0, I).
        Gradient flows through μ and σ, not through the sample.
        """
        if self.training:
            std = torch.exp(0.5 * log_var)
            eps = torch.randn_like(std)
            return mu + std * eps
        else:
            return mu  # use mean at test time for deterministic reconstruction

    def decode(self, z: torch.Tensor):
        """Decode latent code to reconstruction."""
        return self.decoder(z)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor):
        mu, log_var = self.encode(x)
        z           = self.reparametrize(mu, log_var)
        x_hat       = self.decode(z)
        return x_hat, mu, log_var

# ─── Loss ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def elbo_loss(x: torch.Tensor, x_hat: torch.Tensor,
              mu: torch.Tensor, log_var: torch.Tensor,
              beta: float = 1.0) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Negative ELBO = Reconstruction loss + β × KL divergence.

    Reconstruction: -E_q[log p(x|z)] approximated by binary cross-entropy.
    KL: D_KL(N(μ, σ²) || N(0, 1)) = ½ Σ (σ² + μ² - 1 - log σ²)
                                    = ½ Σ (exp(log_var) + μ² - 1 - log_var)
    """
    # Reconstruction loss (sum over pixels, mean over batch)
    recon_loss = F.binary_cross_entropy(x_hat, x, reduction="sum") / x.size(0)

    # KL divergence (closed form for diagonal Gaussian vs. N(0,I))
    kl = 0.5 * (log_var.exp() + mu.pow(2) - 1 - log_var).sum(dim=1).mean()

    return recon_loss + beta * kl

# ─── Training loop ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def train(model: VAE, loader: DataLoader, optimizer: torch.optim.Optimizer,
          device: torch.device, beta: float = 1.0) -> float:
    model.train()
    total_loss = 0.0
    for x, _ in loader:
        x = x.view(x.size(0), -1).to(device)   # flatten 28×28 → 784
        x_hat, mu, log_var = model(x)
        loss = elbo_loss(x, x_hat, mu, log_var, beta=beta)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        total_loss += loss.item()
    return total_loss / len(loader)

# ─── Quick run ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def main():
    device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
    transform = transforms.ToTensor()

    train_ds = datasets.MNIST("./data", train=True,  download=True, transform=transform)
    loader   = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=128, shuffle=True, num_workers=2)

    model     = VAE(x_dim=784, hidden_dim=400, z_dim=20).to(device)
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)

    print("Training VAE for 10 epochs...")
    for epoch in range(1, 11):
        avg_loss = train(model, loader, optimizer, device, beta=1.0)
        print(f"Epoch {epoch:2d} | ELBO loss: {avg_loss:.2f}")

    # ── Generate new samples from prior p(z) = N(0, I) ──
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        z      = torch.randn(16, model.z_dim).to(device)
        x_gen  = model.decode(z)
        x_gen  = x_gen.view(16, 1, 28, 28).cpu()
    print(f"Generated {x_gen.shape[0]} samples. Shape: {x_gen.shape}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

The HierarchicalVAE (NVAE, VDVAE) architecture exemplifies how variational distributions are used in state-of-the-art generative models. Vahdat and Kautz's NVAE (2020) achieved SOTA image generation on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CelebA by using a hierarchical chain of variational distributions: each layer of the hierarchy has its own encoder and variational distribution, conditioned on both the input and the higher-level latent variables.
Below is a simplified two-level hierarchical VAE showing how nested variational distributions work:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class TwoLevelHierarchicalVAE(nn.Module):
    """
    Simplified 2-level hierarchical VAE.
    Two variational distributions: q(z1|x) and q(z2|z1, x).
    ELBO = E[log p(x|z1,z2)] - KL(q(z1|x)||p(z1)) - KL(q(z2|z1,x)||p(z2|z1))
    """
    def __init__(self, x_dim=784, h_dim=256, z1_dim=32, z2_dim=8):
        super().__init__()

        # Bottom-up encoder: x → h
        self.enc_bottom = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(x_dim, h_dim), nn.ReLU())

        # First latent level: q(z1|x) = N(μ1(x), σ1²(x))
        self.mu1      = nn.Linear(h_dim, z1_dim)
        self.lv1      = nn.Linear(h_dim, z1_dim)

        # Second latent level: q(z2|z1) = N(μ2(z1), σ2²(z1))
        self.enc_top  = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(z1_dim, h_dim), nn.ReLU())
        self.mu2      = nn.Linear(h_dim, z2_dim)
        self.lv2      = nn.Linear(h_dim, z2_dim)

        # Decoder: (z1, z2) → x
        self.decoder  = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(z1_dim + z2_dim, h_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(h_dim, x_dim),
            nn.Sigmoid(),
        )

    def encode(self, x):
        h  = self.enc_bottom(x)
        mu1, lv1 = self.mu1(h), self.lv1(h)
        z1 = mu1 + torch.randn_like(mu1) * torch.exp(0.5 * lv1)

        h2 = self.enc_top(z1)
        mu2, lv2 = self.mu2(h2), self.lv2(h2)
        z2 = mu2 + torch.randn_like(mu2) * torch.exp(0.5 * lv2)
        return z1, z2, mu1, lv1, mu2, lv2

    def decode(self, z1, z2):
        return self.decoder(torch.cat([z1, z2], dim=1))

    def forward(self, x):
        z1, z2, mu1, lv1, mu2, lv2 = self.encode(x)
        x_hat = self.decode(z1, z2)
        # ELBO = recon - KL1 - KL2
        recon = F.binary_cross_entropy(x_hat, x, reduction="sum") / x.size(0)
        kl1   = 0.5 * (lv1.exp() + mu1**2 - 1 - lv1).sum(1).mean()
        kl2   = 0.5 * (lv2.exp() + mu2**2 - 1 - lv2).sum(1).mean()
        return recon + kl1 + kl2

model = TwoLevelHierarchicalVAE()
x = torch.rand(32, 784)
loss = model(x)
print(f"2-level HVAE loss: {loss.item():.3f}")
overflows to inf. Clamp log_var in a reasonable range (e.g., [-10, 10]) for numerical stability:
Computing KL in the wrong direction

: variational inference minimizes the reverse KL divergence D_KL(q||p), not the forward KL D_KL(p||q). The closed-form KL for diagonal Gaussians uses q's parameters — not p's. Using p's parameters in the formula gives an incorrect objective.
Not masking out the sampling in KL

: the KL term should be computed using the distribution parameters (μ, log σ²) directly — not from samples. Computing KL from Monte Carlo samples of z is a high-variance estimator; the closed-form KL for Gaussian is exact and should always be used when available.
averages over both the batch and pixel dimensions. This makes the reconstruction loss scale inversely with image size, causing the KL and reconstruction terms to be poorly balanced. Use
and divide only by the batch size.

: in VAE-based text generation (text VAE), the decoder (an LSTM or transformer) is powerful enough to model text without using z at all, causing q to collapse to the prior. Always apply KL annealing or free bits for text VAEs.
Use KL annealing for training stability

: start with a beta of 0 (pure autoencoder, no KL pressure) and linearly anneal to 1 over the first 20–50% of training epochs. This allows the reconstruction to stabilize before applying the regularization pressure that can cause collapse.
: log both components of the ELBO during training. A KL of exactly 0 signals posterior collapse; a KL that grows unboundedly may indicate the reconstruction term is too strong.

: initializing the log_var head with near-zero weights means the initial approximate posterior is close to N(0,I) — matching the prior. This avoids a large initial KL penalty that can destabilize early training.
Use batch normalization or layer normalization in the encoder
: helps stabilize training and prevents log_var from growing too large.
Evaluate with both ELBO and FID

: ELBO measures the tightness of the variational approximation; FID (Fréchet Inception Distance) measures the quality of generated samples. A lower ELBO does not always mean better generation quality — always use both metrics.
: when the latent variable is discrete (categorical), replace the Gaussian reparametrisation with Gumbel-Softmax (concrete distribution):
, where tau is a temperature annealed toward 0 during training.

VAEs are significantly faster to train and sample from than diffusion models and normalizing flows. Training a VAE on MNIST (60K images, 784 dimensions, 20-dim latent) takes under 5 minutes on a CPU. Sampling requires a single forward pass through the decoder — O(L) in the number of decoder layers, compared to O(T×L) for diffusion models with T denoising steps.

For high-resolution images, the bottleneck is the decoder capacity. NVAE (Vahdat & Kautz 2020) scales to 256×256 CelebA with a 30-level hierarchical latent structure by using depthwise separable convolutions and residual cells, achieving an ELBO of 2.91 bits/dim on CIFAR-10 — competitive with flow-based models.
The VAE ELBO provides a bound on the negative log-likelihood:
Tighter bounds are achievable with importance-weighted autoencoders (IWAE), which use K samples to reduce the gap:
With K=50, IWAE achieves bounds within 0.05 bits/dim of the exact log-likelihood on MNIST, compared to ~0.5 bits/dim for standard VAE with K=1.
provides Gaussian, Categorical, and Gumbel-Softmax distributions with built-in KL computation via

: probabilistic programming library built on PyTorch with first-class support for variational inference via the SVI (Stochastic Variational Inference) API; handles the ELBO, guide (variational distribution), and sampling automatically.
: JAX-based probabilistic programming language with highly optimized VI; supports NUTS (MCMC) as an alternative to VI for comparison.
: a benchmark library for evaluating disentangled representation learning methods including beta-VAE, FactorVAE, and MIG score; standardizes comparison across methods.
Does not scale to large datasets

: VAE latent spaces are smooth and continuous by design. Adversarial examples crafted by perturbing the latent code can generate misleading reconstructions. In face generation applications, adversarially crafted z vectors can systematically alter protected attributes. Monitor latent space geometry in sensitive applications.

: VAEs tend to generate blurry or averaged reconstructions, especially when the decoder is underpowered. In medical imaging, blurry reconstructions may omit clinically significant features (lesions, masses). Always validate generative models against domain expert ground truth.

: monitor the KL term, reconstruction loss, and total ELBO separately during training. A sudden spike in KL with a drop in reconstruction often signals that the training has encountered a degenerate configuration. Use gradient clipping (max_norm=1.0) to prevent gradient explosions.

: VAEs trained on private data can partially memorize training examples, especially with small datasets. A generated sample may be close to a training example in pixel space. Apply differential privacy or test for membership inference before deploying models trained on sensitive data.
q_φ(z|x) is a tractable approximation to the intractable true posterior p(z|x), chosen to be simple enough to optimize while rich enough to approximate the true posterior.
(diagonal Gaussian) is the most common choice: it fully factorises the posterior across latent dimensions, making the KL divergence analytically computable.
trains a single encoder network to map any input x to variational parameters, replacing expensive per-sample optimization with one forward pass.
(z = μ + σ⊙ε, ε ~ N(0,I)) makes sampling differentiable, enabling gradient-based optimization of the ELBO through stochastic latent samples.
— where q collapses to the prior and the latent code carries no information — is the most important failure mode; mitigate with KL annealing, free bits, or architectural constraints on the decoder.
provides a computable lower bound on log p(x); tighter bounds are available via importance-weighted autoencoders (IWAE) with K > 1 samples.
VAEs train faster and sample faster than diffusion models but produce lower-quality samples; they offer a structured, continuous latent space useful for interpolation and uncertainty quantification.
